rows.name: Wheat / wheat flour
rows.what: Cereal grain — primary gluten source in UK diet
rows.kip: Direct gluten source. Bread, pasta, cakes, biscuits, breakfast cereals, sauces, soy sauce.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Durum / durum wheat / semolina
rows.what: Hard wheat used for pasta and couscous
rows.kip: Direct gluten source. Pasta, couscous, some breads, semolina puddings.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Kamut / khorasan
rows.kip: Contains gluten. Not safe for coeliac.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Einkorn
rows.kip: Contains gluten. Lower gluten content than modern wheat but still not coeliac-safe.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Triticale
rows.kip: Contains gluten from both parents.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Rye
rows.kip: Direct gluten source. Rye bread, crispbreads, pumpernickel.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Barley
rows.what: Cereal grain — used in beer, malt, soups
rows.kip: Direct gluten source. Beer, malt, malt vinegar, soups, stews.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Malt / malt extract / barley malt
rows.kip: Contains gluten unless specifically labelled gluten-free. Common in breakfast cereals, malt loaves, beers.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Bulgur wheat
rows.kip: Direct gluten source.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Couscous
rows.kip: Direct gluten source.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Farro / emmer
rows.kip: Contains gluten.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Vital wheat gluten / wheat gluten
rows.kip: Pure gluten. Used as a meat substitute (seitan) and to strengthen bread dough.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Seitan
rows.kip: Almost pure gluten. Common in plant-based meat alternatives.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Hydrolysed wheat protein

rows.kip: Direct wheat/gluten source. Common in soups, sauces, ready meals.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Wheatgerm / wheat bran
rows.kip: Contain gluten. Used in cereals and baking.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Malt vinegar
rows.kip: Coeliac UK considers malt vinegar safe for coeliacs because the gluten level is below 20ppm after fermentation. People with wheat allergy may still need to avoid it.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Prawns / shrimp
rows.kip: Direct source. Common in stir-fries, curries, sandwiches, prawn crackers.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Crab
rows.kip: Direct source. Crab sticks, dressed crab, crab paste, sushi.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Lobster

rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Conalbumin / mucin / vitellin

rows.name: Fish oil
rows.kip: Direct fish source. Used in supplements and fortified foods.

rows.name: Surimi

rows.name: Peanut oil / groundnut oil / arachis oil
rows.kip: Highly refined peanut oil is generally tolerated by peanut-allergic individuals; cold-pressed/unrefined peanut oil retains protein and is unsafe. The label rarely distinguishes — assume it does unless certain.

rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Tahini / sesame paste
rows.kip: Direct sesame source. Common in hummus, baba ghanoush.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sesame oil (toasted or untoasted)
rows.kip: Direct sesame source. Generally retains protein.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Halva / halawi
rows.kip: Direct sesame source.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Furikake / gomashio
rows.kip: Direct sesame source.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Hummus / houmous
rows.kip: Always contains sesame (via tahini). Direct source.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sulphur dioxide (E220)
rows.kip: Direct source. Used in dried fruit, wine, beer, processed meats. Asthma trigger in sensitive individuals.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium sulphite (E221)
rows.kip: Direct sulphite source.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium bisulphite / sodium hydrogen sulphite (E222)
rows.kip: Direct sulphite source. Common in wine and dried fruit.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium metabisulphite (E223)
rows.kip: Direct sulphite source. Common in wine, dried fruit, sausages, prawns.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Potassium metabisulphite (E224)
rows.kip: Direct sulphite source.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Calcium sulphite (E226)
rows.kip: Direct sulphite source.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Calcium hydrogen sulphite (E227)
rows.kip: Direct sulphite source.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Potassium bisulphite (E228)
rows.kip: Direct sulphite source.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: "Contains sulphites"
rows.kip: UK labelling threshold — sulphites must be declared above 10 ppm. Below that, no declaration is required.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Lupin / lupine
rows.what: Legume — flour, seed, or whole bean
rows.kip: Direct source. Used in pasta, gluten-free baked goods, vegan protein products. Cross-reactive with peanut for some allergic individuals.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Lupin flour / lupin seed flour
rows.kip: Direct lupin source. Used as a flour substitute in some gluten-free products.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Lupin protein / lupin protein isolate
rows.kip: Direct lupin source. Used in plant-based foods.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Tremoço / lupini beans
rows.kip: Direct lupin source. Mediterranean snack.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Mussels
rows.kip: Direct source.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Oysters
rows.kip: Direct source.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Clams / cockles
rows.kip: Direct source.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Scallops
rows.kip: Direct source.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Squid / calamari / cuttlefish
rows.kip: Direct mollusc source.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Octopus
rows.kip: Direct mollusc source.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Snails / escargot
rows.kip: Direct mollusc source.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Whelks / winkles
rows.kip: Direct mollusc source. Traditional UK seaside snack.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Oyster sauce / oyster extract
rows.kip: Direct mollusc source. Common in Asian sauces, stir-fry mixes.
rows.page_title: The 14 UK Allergens, Decoded — Hidden Names on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Manufacturer code (variable length)
rows.category: EAN-13 anatomy — what each part means
rows.what: Unique identifier for the company that registered the product
rows.kip: The number of digits is variable (typically 4–7) depending on how many products the manufacturer needs to register. Larger manufacturers get shorter codes (more product-code digits remaining).
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Prefix 0–1 — USA & Canada
rows.category: GS1 country prefixes — most common on UK shelves
rows.what: Issued by GS1 US and GS1 Canada
rows.kip: On UPC-A barcodes (12 digits), the prefix is 0–1 (encoded as the leading 0 in EAN-13 form). Common on US imports.
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Prefix 30–37 — France
rows.category: GS1 country prefixes — most common on UK shelves
rows.kip: Common on French-imported products. Cheese, charcuterie, mineral water, biscuits, wines and spirits often carry French-registered barcodes.
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Prefix 40–44 — Germany
rows.category: GS1 country prefixes — most common on UK shelves
rows.kip: Common on German-imported products. Aldi and Lidl own-brand often carry German prefixes (the parent companies registered the barcodes there).
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Prefix 80–83 — Italy
rows.category: GS1 country prefixes — most common on UK shelves
rows.kip: Common on Italian foods (pasta, olive oil, tomato passata, charcuterie).
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Prefix 84 — Spain
rows.category: GS1 country prefixes — most common on UK shelves
rows.kip: Common on Spanish foods (charcuterie, olives, oranges, tinned fish).
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Prefix 87 — Netherlands
rows.category: GS1 country prefixes — most common on UK shelves
rows.kip: Common on Dutch-registered FMCG — Unilever HQ products often carry this prefix.
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Prefix 690–699 — China
rows.category: GS1 country prefixes — most common on UK shelves
rows.kip: On Chinese-registered products. Widely seen on imports across categories from condiments to processed snacks.
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Prefix 471 — Taiwan
rows.category: GS1 country prefixes — most common on UK shelves
rows.kip: Common on Taiwanese-registered products (frequently mistaken for a Chinese prefix; Taiwan has its own range).
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Prefix 489 — Hong Kong
rows.category: GS1 country prefixes — most common on UK shelves
rows.what: Issued by GS1 Hong Kong, China
rows.kip: Common on East Asian imports registered through Hong Kong.
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Prefix 880 — South Korea
rows.category: GS1 country prefixes — most common on UK shelves
rows.kip: On Korean-registered products. Common on K-food imports (kimchi, gochujang, instant noodles, snacks).
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Prefix 893 — Vietnam
rows.category: GS1 country prefixes — most common on UK shelves
rows.kip: Common on Vietnamese-registered products (fish sauce, noodles, sauces, coffee, tea).
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Prefix 020–029 — restricted (in-store)
rows.category: GS1 country prefixes — most common on UK shelves
rows.kip: Used by supermarket scales for fresh meat, fish, deli, and produce sold by weight. The barcode is generated in-store at the point of weighing; the digits encode the weight or price rather than a global product identifier. Not a manufacturer-registered barcode.
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Prefix 200–299 — restricted (in-store)
rows.category: GS1 country prefixes — most common on UK shelves
rows.kip: Same use as 020–029 — in-store generated barcodes for variable-weight or in-store-prepared items.
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Prefix 977 — periodicals (ISSN)
rows.category: GS1 country prefixes — most common on UK shelves
rows.kip: Magazines and journals. Not food.
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Prefix 978–979 — books (ISBN / ISMN)
rows.category: GS1 country prefixes — most common on UK shelves
rows.kip: Books carry an ISBN encoded as an EAN-13 with the 978 or 979 prefix. Not food — useful as a reminder that the prefix range tells you the product class, not the country.
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Prefix 981–984 — coupons
rows.category: GS1 country prefixes — most common on UK shelves
rows.kip: Vouchers and money-off coupons. Not food.
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Which national region the manufacturer registered in
rows.what: The prefix identifies a national GS1 body, not a manufacturing country
rows.kip: The most-misread piece of information on a food pack. Useful as a registration signal; not useful as country-of-origin.
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: That the product version is distinct
rows.what: Different sizes / flavours / formats have different barcodes
rows.kip: Useful for tracking reformulation. If a manufacturer reformulates a product without changing the barcode, scanners will return the old data. Watching for new barcodes is one signal that a product has been reformulated.
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: \"Sunrise 2027\" — GS1 transition
rows.category: Beyond linear — QR codes and smart labels
rows.what: Industry target for retailers to handle 2D codes at the till
rows.kip: GS1 is encouraging retailers to upgrade till scanners to read 2D codes (QR, Data Matrix) by 2027 alongside or instead of linear barcodes. The transition is voluntary; large UK retailers are at varying stages of readiness.
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: EU Digital Product Passport (DPP)
rows.category: Beyond linear — QR codes and smart labels
rows.what: EU regulatory framework for product traceability via 2D code
rows.kip: Covers textiles, batteries, electronics first; food is on the regulatory agenda. The DPP framework specifies a QR or Data Matrix on every covered product carrying structured product information accessible to consumers and regulators.
rows.page_title: Barcodes, Decoded — What the Lines and Numbers Actually Mean · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: kcal (kilocalorie)
rows.what: The unit UK shoppers usually read
rows.kip: Often just called \"calorie\" in casual usage. 1 kcal = the energy needed to raise 1 kg of water by 1°C. Mandatory on every UK nutrition declaration alongside kJ.
rows.page_title: Calories & Energy, Decoded — kJ vs kcal, Reference Intake, Per-Gram Conversions · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Per 100g vs per portion
rows.what: Two columns on every nutrition declaration
rows.kip: The per-100g column lets you compare any two products. The per-portion column depends on what the manufacturer chose to call a portion — portion sizes are not standardised across UK food categories. A small bag of crisps and a sharing bag declare different per-portion calories despite the same kcal/100g. Compare per-100g, not per-portion.
rows.page_title: Calories & Energy, Decoded — kJ vs kcal, Reference Intake, Per-Gram Conversions · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Reference Intake (RI) for energy
rows.what: 2,000 kcal / 8,400 kJ — the average adult standard
rows.kip: All \"% RI\" calculations use this as the denominator. Men typically eat 2,500 kcal; women, 2,000; children, considerably less. The RI is a relative scale, not a personal target.
rows.page_title: Calories & Energy, Decoded — kJ vs kcal, Reference Intake, Per-Gram Conversions · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Carbohydrates — 4 kcal per gram
rows.kip: Sugars and starches both deliver about 4 kcal per gram. A 100g portion of cooked rice (around 28g carbs) is around 130 kcal — the rest is water.
rows.page_title: Calories & Energy, Decoded — kJ vs kcal, Reference Intake, Per-Gram Conversions · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Fat — 9 kcal per gram
rows.what: More than twice the energy density of protein or carbs
rows.kip: This is why fatty foods are energy-dense. A tablespoon of olive oil (around 14g fat) is around 120 kcal. A 30g pack of crisps (around 10g fat) gets most of its calories from the fat, not the potato.
rows.page_title: Calories & Energy, Decoded — kJ vs kcal, Reference Intake, Per-Gram Conversions · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Alcohol — 7 kcal per gram
rows.what: Energy yield per gram of pure alcohol (ethanol)
rows.kip: Higher than protein or carbs, lower than fat. A pint of 5% beer (around 568 ml, ~20g alcohol) is around 140 kcal from alcohol alone, plus carbs from the malt. UK CMO guidance is ≤14 units per week; 1 unit = 8g alcohol = 56 kcal.
rows.page_title: Calories & Energy, Decoded — kJ vs kcal, Reference Intake, Per-Gram Conversions · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Fibre — 2 kcal per gram
rows.what: Reduced absorption gives fibre about half the energy of carbs
rows.kip: FIR Annex XIV permits the use of 2 kcal/g for fibre in nutrition declarations. Insoluble fibre delivers slightly less; soluble slightly more. Most labels use 2 kcal/g consistently.
rows.page_title: Calories & Energy, Decoded — kJ vs kcal, Reference Intake, Per-Gram Conversions · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Salatrim / olestra / fat replacers
rows.what: Reduced-calorie fat substitutes — specific kcal/g values
rows.kip: Industrial fat replacers used in low-fat versions of biscuits, snacks, dairy. Calorie yields range from 0–6 kcal/g depending on the substance. Rare in UK retail.
rows.page_title: Calories & Energy, Decoded — kJ vs kcal, Reference Intake, Per-Gram Conversions · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: \"Low calorie\" / \"low energy\"
rows.kip: Strict per-100g/ml threshold. Note the per-portion figure can be very different if the portion is small or large.
rows.page_title: Calories & Energy, Decoded — kJ vs kcal, Reference Intake, Per-Gram Conversions · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: \"Energy free\" / \"calorie free\"
rows.kip: Reserved for drinks. Diet sodas typically meet this threshold. Solid foods cannot use this claim.
rows.page_title: Calories & Energy, Decoded — kJ vs kcal, Reference Intake, Per-Gram Conversions · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Menu calorie display (since April 2022)
rows.kip: UK Calorie Labelling (Out-of-Home Sector) Regulations 2021. Calories per item must appear on menus, online ordering, delivery platforms, and printed displays. Smaller businesses (under 250 employees) are exempt.
rows.page_title: Calories & Energy, Decoded — kJ vs kcal, Reference Intake, Per-Gram Conversions · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: \"Adults need around 2,000 kcal a day\"
rows.what: Mandatory accompanying statement on out-of-home menus
rows.kip: Required on every menu carrying calorie counts. Provides context for the displayed numbers.
rows.page_title: Calories & Energy, Decoded — kJ vs kcal, Reference Intake, Per-Gram Conversions · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Exemptions to out-of-home calorie display
rows.what: Special-occasion items, custom orders, daily-changing items, condiments
rows.kip: Daily-changing specials, off-menu items, condiments, and free-of-charge tastings are exempt. Schools and hospitals are not covered by these specific regulations (different rules apply).
rows.page_title: Calories & Energy, Decoded — kJ vs kcal, Reference Intake, Per-Gram Conversions · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Calories per unit of alcohol
rows.what: 1 UK alcohol unit = 8g alcohol = 56 kcal from alcohol alone
rows.kip: This is calories from the alcohol itself; mixers, sugars, and carbohydrates add more. A pint of 5% lager is around 140 kcal total. A 175ml glass of 12% wine is around 130 kcal. A 25ml shot of 40% spirit is around 56 kcal (pure alcohol; mixers add more).
rows.page_title: Calories & Energy, Decoded — kJ vs kcal, Reference Intake, Per-Gram Conversions · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Drinkaware unit and calorie guidance
rows.kip: Drinkaware.co.uk publishes per-drink unit and calorie estimates. UK CMO guidance is ≤14 units per week, spread across at least three days.
rows.page_title: Calories & Energy, Decoded — kJ vs kcal, Reference Intake, Per-Gram Conversions · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Country flag / national imagery
rows.what: Use of UK or country flags as origin signal
rows.kip: Use of a Union Jack, country flag, or other strongly identifying imagery on the front of pack triggers the FIR Article 7 rule against misleading origin information. If imagery suggests origin, the product must be from that place — or the actual origin must be made equally prominent.
rows.page_title: Country of Origin, Decoded — Made in vs Produce of vs Packed in · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: FAO catch area
rows.what: For wild-caught fish, the FAO statistical fishing area must be declared
rows.kip: FAO area numbers (e.g., FAO 27 = North-East Atlantic; FAO 37 = Mediterranean and Black Sea; FAO 71 = Western Central Pacific). Required under retained EU 1379/2013.
rows.page_title: Country of Origin, Decoded — Made in vs Produce of vs Packed in · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Catch gear
rows.what: For wild-caught fish, the gear category may be declared
rows.kip: Categories defined by Annex III of 1379/2013: seines, trawls, gillnets, surrounding nets, lift nets, hooks and lines, dredges, pots and traps. Voluntary on most retail packs but mandatory on certain export markets.
rows.page_title: Country of Origin, Decoded — Made in vs Produce of vs Packed in · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: PDO — Protected Designation of Origin
rows.what: Strictest GI: production, processing, and preparation all in the named region
rows.kip: All stages (raw materials, production, processing, preparation) must take place in the named region. UK PDOs include West Country Farmhouse Cheddar, Stilton, Yorkshire Forced Rhubarb, Single Gloucester cheese.
rows.page_title: Country of Origin, Decoded — Made in vs Produce of vs Packed in · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: PGI — Protected Geographical Indication
rows.what: At least one stage of production, processing, or preparation must take place in the region
rows.kip: Less strict than PDO. UK PGIs include Cornish Pasty, Melton Mowbray Pork Pie, Scotch Beef, Welsh Lamb.
rows.page_title: Country of Origin, Decoded — Made in vs Produce of vs Packed in · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: UK GI logo (post-Brexit)
rows.what: Distinct UK logo for the post-Brexit UK GI scheme
rows.kip: From 2024, UK GI products use a distinct UK logo alongside (or replacing) the EU PDO/PGI/TSG logos. Existing EU GIs continue to be recognised in UK; UK GIs are increasingly applying for separate EU recognition.
rows.page_title: Country of Origin, Decoded — Made in vs Produce of vs Packed in · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Use by
rows.what: Safety line for highly perishable food
rows.kip: Required by law on highly perishable foods. After this date the product may be unsafe to eat. Cooking does not necessarily make use-by-expired food safe. Honour this date. Freezing on the day of expiry extends the safe window if the food has been stored correctly.
rows.page_title: Date Labels, Decoded — Use By vs Best Before · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Best before
rows.what: Quality line for food with longer shelf life
rows.kip: Required by law on most ambient, dried, frozen, and tinned products where quality (not safety) declines over time. Past the date the food is generally still safe but may have changed in taste, texture, or appearance. Use your eyes, nose, and judgement. Eggs are a notable case — UK rules require best before for eggs at 28 days from lay.
rows.page_title: Date Labels, Decoded — Use By vs Best Before · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Display until
rows.kip: Voluntary retailer instruction. Tells shop staff when to take a product off the shelf. Has no legal meaning as consumer information. The FSA recommends consumers ignore display-until dates and look at use-by or best-before instead.
rows.page_title: Date Labels, Decoded — Use By vs Best Before · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sell by
rows.kip: Voluntary retailer instruction. Same logic as display-until. Largely phased out by major UK retailers to reduce shopper confusion.
rows.page_title: Date Labels, Decoded — Use By vs Best Before · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Packed on
rows.kip: Voluntary information. Useful for fresh produce and butchery counters but not legally meaningful as consumer information. Distinct from harvest or slaughter date.
rows.page_title: Date Labels, Decoded — Use By vs Best Before · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Once opened, consume within X days
rows.kip: Often shorter than the printed use-by date. After a pack is opened, the printed use-by may no longer apply — the post-opening period takes over. Common on cooked meats, soft cheese, dressings.
rows.page_title: Date Labels, Decoded — Use By vs Best Before · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Suitable for home freezing
rows.what: Permission to freeze beyond the use-by date
rows.kip: You can freeze the product right up to the use-by date and consume it within recommended frozen storage. Once defrosted, use within 24 hours unless re-cooked.
rows.page_title: Date Labels, Decoded — Use By vs Best Before · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Do not refreeze once thawed
rows.what: Safety instruction for previously frozen food
rows.kip: Refreezing thawed food increases bacterial-growth opportunity. Cook the thawed food, then freeze the cooked product if needed.
rows.page_title: Date Labels, Decoded — Use By vs Best Before · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Use frozen by [date]
rows.kip: Quality (not safety) marker for products bought frozen. Frozen storage at −18°C is microbiologically very safe; the date refers to texture and flavour quality.
rows.page_title: Date Labels, Decoded — Use By vs Best Before · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Keep refrigerated
rows.kip: Required for products that need temperatures below 8°C (UK standard) to remain safe. Most home fridges should run at 0–5°C.
rows.page_title: Date Labels, Decoded — Use By vs Best Before · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Store in a cool, dry place
rows.kip: Generally below 25°C, away from direct sunlight. Quality marker, not a safety threshold for shelf-stable food.
rows.page_title: Date Labels, Decoded — Use By vs Best Before · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Egg code (e.g., 0UK12345)
rows.kip: First digit: 0=organic, 1=free range, 2=barn, 3=cage. Then country code (UK), then producer ID. Required on UK Class A eggs sold in shell.
rows.page_title: Date Labels, Decoded — Use By vs Best Before · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Butter
rows.what: Dairy fat, around 80% saturated and unsaturated fat with around 50% saturated
rows.kip: High in saturated fat. Around 51g saturated per 100g. UK NHS guidance is to limit saturated fat overall.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Lard
rows.kip: Around 39g saturated per 100g. Used in pastry, frying, and traditional baking. Contributes to daily saturated total.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Beef tallow
rows.kip: Around 50g saturated per 100g. Sometimes used in commercial frying and baked goods.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Suet
rows.what: Hard fat from around the kidneys of beef or mutton
rows.kip: Very high saturated fat. Used in puddings, dumplings, mincemeat. Vegetable suet is a non-animal alternative with similar saturated content.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Dripping
rows.kip: Around 50g saturated per 100g. Traditional cooking fat.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Ghee
rows.kip: Around 62g saturated per 100g — higher than butter once water and milk solids are removed. Common in South Asian cooking.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Cream
rows.what: Dairy cream — fat content varies by type
rows.kip: Single 18% fat, whipping 35%, double 48%, clotted 55%. Most of that is saturated.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Milk fat / butter fat
rows.what: Dairy fat in milk and dairy ingredients
rows.kip: Predominantly saturated. Listed on labels of yoghurts, cheeses, ice cream and processed dairy products.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Anhydrous milk fat (AMF)
rows.what: Concentrated dairy fat — water and protein removed
rows.kip: Almost pure milk fat. Used in confectionery, ice cream, and bakery for richness and texture.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Palm oil
rows.what: Oil from the fruit of the African oil palm
rows.kip: Around 50g saturated per 100g. Used in roughly half of UK supermarket products. Also a major sustainability concern — RSPO certification flags responsibly sourced versions.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Palm kernel oil
rows.what: Oil from the seed (kernel) of the oil palm — different from palm oil
rows.kip: Around 82g saturated per 100g — much higher than palm oil. Used in confectionery coatings and creamy textures.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Palm olein
rows.what: The liquid fraction of palm oil after partial crystallisation
rows.kip: Lower in saturated fat than palm oil overall but still 40%+ saturated. Used as a frying oil.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Palm stearin
rows.what: The solid fraction of palm oil — high saturated content
rows.kip: Around 53g saturated per 100g. Used in margarines, shortenings, and as a hard-fat ingredient.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Palm fat
rows.what: General term for palm-derived fat — usually palm oil or fractions
rows.kip: A non-specific name. Could be palm oil, palm olein, palm stearin, or a blend. Check if a more specific name appears alongside.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Hydrogenated palm oil
rows.what: Palm oil that has been chemically hardened
rows.kip: If fully hydrogenated, very high saturated fat but no industrial trans. If partially hydrogenated, may contain industrial trans fats — restricted in UK to 2g per 100g of fat.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Coconut oil
rows.kip: Around 87g saturated per 100g. Higher saturated content than butter or lard. Marketed as healthful — Public Health England and the BHF advise treating it like other saturated fats.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Coconut cream
rows.what: Concentrated coconut fat with reduced water
rows.kip: Very high saturated. Used in curries and desserts.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Cocoa butter
rows.kip: Around 60g saturated per 100g — but the saturated fat is dominated by stearic acid, which the body handles differently from other saturates. Used in chocolate (a legal requirement for milk and dark chocolate).
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sal fat / illipe / mango kernel fat
rows.what: Cocoa butter equivalents (CBEs) — vegetable fats permitted up to 5% in chocolate
rows.kip: Permitted by UK Cocoa and Chocolate Products Regulations as substitutes for up to 5% of cocoa butter. Must be declared on the label if used.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Vegetable oil (unspecified)
rows.kip: A general term. Could be sunflower, rapeseed, soya, palm, or a blend. Specific oils must be named on UK labels (FIR Article 18).
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Palmitic acid
rows.what: Most common saturated fatty acid — abundant in palm oil and animal fats
rows.kip: The dominant fatty acid in palm oil. Raises LDL cholesterol per the Mensink meta-analysis.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Lauric acid
rows.what: Saturated fatty acid abundant in coconut and palm kernel
rows.kip: Raises both LDL and HDL cholesterol. Found in coconut oil and palm kernel oil.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Myristic acid
rows.what: Saturated fatty acid in dairy and tropical oils
rows.kip: One of the saturates most associated with raising LDL cholesterol. Found in butter, coconut oil, palm kernel oil.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Olive oil — extra virgin
rows.what: First cold-pressed olive oil — chemical-free extraction
rows.kip: Around 73% monounsaturated. The most lightly processed olive oil. Acidity below 0.8% by EU/UK regulation.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Olive oil — virgin
rows.what: Cold-pressed olive oil with slightly higher acidity
rows.kip: Around 73% monounsaturated. Acidity below 2%. Less commonly retailed in the UK.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Olive pomace oil
rows.what: Oil extracted from olive residue using solvents and heat
rows.kip: Lowest grade of olive oil. Same fatty acid composition; flavour is neutral; used commercially. Must be labelled as pomace.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: High-oleic sunflower oil
rows.what: Sunflower oil bred for higher monounsaturated content
rows.kip: Around 80%+ monounsaturated. More heat-stable than regular sunflower oil. Common in commercial frying.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Avocado oil
rows.kip: Around 70% monounsaturated. High smoke point. Marketed at premium price points.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Groundnut / peanut oil
rows.kip: Around 49% monounsaturated, 33% polyunsaturated, 18% saturated. Allergen — must be declared.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Almond oil
rows.kip: Around 70% monounsaturated. Allergen (tree nut) — must be declared.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Hazelnut oil
rows.kip: Around 79% monounsaturated. Strong flavour — used as a finishing oil. Allergen — must be declared.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sunflower oil
rows.kip: Around 65% polyunsaturated, mostly omega-6 linoleic acid. Common cooking and frying oil. Heat-stable up to medium-high temperatures.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Soybean oil
rows.kip: Around 58% polyunsaturated. Common in commercial baking and frying. Allergen (soy) — must be declared.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Corn oil / maize oil
rows.kip: Around 58% polyunsaturated. Common frying oil.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sesame oil
rows.kip: Around 42% polyunsaturated, 40% monounsaturated. Allergen — must be declared. Toasted sesame oil is the dark, finishing version.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Linseed oil / flaxseed oil
rows.kip: Around 58% polyunsaturated, of which roughly 53% is alpha-linolenic acid (ALA, omega-3). One of the highest plant sources of ALA. Not heat-stable.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Hemp seed oil
rows.kip: Around 80% polyunsaturated with a 3:1 omega-6 to omega-3 ratio. Not heat-stable. No detectable THC.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Walnut oil
rows.kip: Around 63% polyunsaturated, 14% omega-3 ALA. Allergen — must be declared. Used as a finishing oil.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Fish oil
rows.what: Oil from oily fish — natural source of EPA and DHA
rows.kip: Long-chain omega-3 (EPA, DHA). Used in supplements and fortified products. Fish allergen — must be declared.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Algae oil
rows.what: Oil from cultivated marine algae — vegetarian DHA and EPA
rows.kip: Plant-based source of long-chain omega-3. Used in supplements and fortified foods.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Cottonseed oil
rows.kip: Around 52% polyunsaturated. Used in commercial frying and baking. Less common in UK retail than the other oils.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Grapeseed oil
rows.what: Oil from grape seeds — by-product of winemaking
rows.kip: Around 70% polyunsaturated. Used in salad dressings and finishing.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Rice bran oil
rows.kip: Around 37% polyunsaturated, 39% monounsaturated. Heat-stable. Used in commercial frying.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Partially hydrogenated vegetable oil
rows.what: Plant oil chemically hardened — produces industrial trans fats
rows.kip: The main source of industrial trans fats. Restricted in UK to 2g per 100g of total fat in food (retained EU Regulation 2019/649). Largely phased out of UK retail; still possible in imports.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Partially hydrogenated soybean oil
rows.what: Soybean oil chemically hardened — industrial trans source
rows.kip: Restricted as above. Allergen (soy) — must be declared.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Partially hydrogenated palm oil
rows.what: Palm oil chemically hardened — industrial trans source
rows.kip: Restricted as above.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Fully hydrogenated vegetable oil
rows.what: Plant oil fully hardened — saturated, no industrial trans
rows.kip: Full hydrogenation produces saturated fat without trans fats. Often combined with a liquid oil to make a target consistency. High saturated content.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Margarine
rows.what: Spread made from emulsified water and vegetable oils
rows.kip: Most modern UK margarines use interesterification or fully hydrogenated blends rather than partial hydrogenation, so industrial trans is typically below 1%. Still high in fat overall — check the label.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Vegetable shortening
rows.kip: Modern UK shortenings are usually based on interesterified or fully hydrogenated fats with low industrial trans. Older formulations relied on partial hydrogenation.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Trans fatty acids (industrial)
rows.what: Trans fats produced by partial hydrogenation
rows.kip: Limited to 2g per 100g of fat in UK food. Linked to raised LDL cholesterol and lowered HDL per Mensink meta-analysis.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Trans fatty acids (ruminant)
rows.what: Naturally occurring trans fats from beef, lamb, dairy
rows.kip: Different chemical structure (mainly vaccenic acid) from industrial trans. Not subject to the 2019/649 regulation. Present at 2-5% of total fat in dairy and beef.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium palmate
rows.what: Sodium salt of palmitic acid — soap and cosmetic ingredient
rows.kip: Often palm-derived. Common in bar soaps. Sustainability concerns.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium palm kernelate
rows.what: Sodium salt from palm kernel oil
rows.kip: Palm kernel-derived. Common in soaps.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium palmitate
rows.what: Sodium salt of palmitic acid — emulsifier and surfactant
rows.kip: Often palm-derived but can come from other sources. Used in soaps and processed foods.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Glyceryl monostearate (E471, palm-derived)
rows.what: Emulsifier — also from soy, sunflower, or animal sources
rows.kip: Source not always specified on label. Major emulsifier in UK bakery, ice cream, and processed dairy.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Cetyl palmitate
rows.what: Wax ester from palmitic acid — cosmetic emollient
rows.kip: Often palm-derived. Common in cosmetics; rare in food.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Glycerin / glycerol (palm-derived)
rows.what: Humectant — also from soy, rapeseed, animal
rows.kip: Source rarely specified. Multi-purpose ingredient: humectant, sweetener, solvent.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Stearyl alcohol
rows.what: Emollient and emulsifier — from saturated fats
rows.kip: Often palm-derived. Used in cosmetics and rarely in food.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Tocopherols (E306, palm-derived)
rows.kip: Often extracted from palm oil distillates. Used as a natural antioxidant.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Mono- and diglycerides of fatty acids (E471)
rows.what: Emulsifier in bakery, dairy, ice cream, ready meals
rows.kip: The most-used food emulsifier in the UK. Often palm-derived but can come from soy, sunflower, or animal sources. Counts toward total fat on the nutrition panel.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Diacetyl tartaric acid esters of mono- and diglycerides (DATEM, E472e)
rows.kip: Standard ingredient in commercial UK bread. Derived from glycerol and tartaric acid esters of fatty acids.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Acetic acid esters of mono- and diglycerides (E472a)
rows.what: Emulsifier in bakery and frozen desserts
rows.kip: Fatty acid derivative. Stable in heating.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Lactic acid esters of mono- and diglycerides (E472b)
rows.what: Emulsifier in cake mixes and whipped toppings
rows.kip: Fatty acid derivative.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Citric acid esters of mono- and diglycerides (E472c)
rows.kip: Fatty acid derivative.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Polyglycerol esters of fatty acids (E475)
rows.what: Emulsifier in chocolate and cake mixes
rows.kip: Fatty acid derivative. Common in confectionery.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sucrose esters of fatty acids (E473)
rows.what: Emulsifier in bakery and dairy alternatives
rows.kip: Sucrose esterified with fatty acids. Common in plant-based products.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sorbitan monostearate (E491)
rows.what: Emulsifier and stabiliser in icing and bakery
rows.kip: Fatty acid ester of sorbitol.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Polyglycerol polyricinoleate (PGPR, E476)
rows.what: Emulsifier in chocolate — reduces cocoa butter requirement
rows.kip: Used in chocolate at low concentrations to improve flow. Allows chocolate manufacturers to use less cocoa butter.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Calcium stearoyl-2-lactylate (E482)
rows.kip: Common in commercial bread.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium stearoyl lactylate (E481)
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing emulsifier. See also our Hidden Names for Salt reference.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Lecithin (E322)
rows.kip: Often soy-derived (allergen — must be declared). Sunflower lecithin is a common alternative. Counts as fat on the nutrition panel.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Interesterified fat / interesterified oil
rows.what: Fat with rearranged fatty acids — alternative to partial hydrogenation
rows.kip: Used to make solid fats without producing industrial trans fats. Saturated fat content unchanged. Long-term cardiovascular evidence still developing.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Fat-modified / fractionated fat
rows.what: Fat split into liquid and solid components
rows.kip: Physical separation of fat fractions for specific functional uses (palm olein and stearin are examples).
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Fats, Decoded — What Fats Really Look Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Amber dot — medium
rows.what: The product is medium in this nutrient at the per-100g threshold
rows.kip: Sits between the FSA low and high thresholds. Most processed food sits amber on at least one nutrient. Not a problem in itself; useful as context relative to the rest of the label.
rows.page_title: Front-of-Pack Systems, Decoded — Traffic Lights, RI, Nutri-Score · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Per-portion thresholds
rows.what: For products consumed as a single portion (single-portion ready meals etc.), per-portion thresholds may apply
rows.kip: For larger single-portion products, the FSA recommends a separate set of per-portion thresholds: fat >21g, saturates >6g, sugars >27g, salt >1.8g (high). The per-portion logic kicks in when one serving exceeds a single 100g equivalent.
rows.page_title: Front-of-Pack Systems, Decoded — Traffic Lights, RI, Nutri-Score · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Saturated fat thresholds
rows.kip: Saturated fat. Most relevant for cardiovascular guidance.
rows.page_title: Front-of-Pack Systems, Decoded — Traffic Lights, RI, Nutri-Score · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sugars thresholds
rows.kip: Total sugars (added + naturally occurring). For a product with substantial natural sugar (e.g., dried fruit), the green/amber/red colour reflects total intake, not just added sugar.
rows.page_title: Front-of-Pack Systems, Decoded — Traffic Lights, RI, Nutri-Score · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Salt thresholds
rows.kip: Salt = sodium × 2.5. The per-100g logic applies even for products eaten in small portions like soy sauce.
rows.page_title: Front-of-Pack Systems, Decoded — Traffic Lights, RI, Nutri-Score · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: % RI for sugars
rows.what: Based on 90g sugars per day for the reference adult
rows.kip: The 90g RI is for total sugars; the 2015 SACN recommendation is 30g of free sugars per day. RIs and free-sugar guidance use different denominators — RI overstates the headroom for free sugars relative to current public-health guidance.
rows.page_title: Front-of-Pack Systems, Decoded — Traffic Lights, RI, Nutri-Score · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: % RI for salt
rows.what: Based on 6g salt per day for the reference adult
rows.kip: NHS guideline. A portion at 25% RI salt contains 1.5g salt — the FSA per-100g high threshold.
rows.page_title: Front-of-Pack Systems, Decoded — Traffic Lights, RI, Nutri-Score · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: % RI for energy
rows.what: Based on 2,000 kcal/8,400 kJ per day
rows.kip: Reference adult. Children aged 1–3: ~1,000 kcal; 4–6: ~1,400; 7–10: ~1,800; teens and adults vary. Personal RI may be higher or lower.
rows.page_title: Front-of-Pack Systems, Decoded — Traffic Lights, RI, Nutri-Score · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Nutri-Score B (light green)
rows.kip: Common for plain dairy, lean protein, lightly processed staples.
rows.page_title: Front-of-Pack Systems, Decoded — Traffic Lights, RI, Nutri-Score · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Nutri-Score C (yellow)
rows.kip: Where most processed food sits.
rows.page_title: Front-of-Pack Systems, Decoded — Traffic Lights, RI, Nutri-Score · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Nutri-Score D (orange)
rows.kip: Higher in unfavourable nutrients relative to favourable.
rows.page_title: Front-of-Pack Systems, Decoded — Traffic Lights, RI, Nutri-Score · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Nutri-Score E (red)
rows.kip: High energy density, sugars, saturates, sodium, with low compensating fibre/protein/fruit content.
rows.page_title: Front-of-Pack Systems, Decoded — Traffic Lights, RI, Nutri-Score · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: UK adoption status
rows.what: Not officially adopted by UK regulators
rows.kip: The UK has not adopted Nutri-Score as a national front-of-pack system. Products carrying Nutri-Score on UK shelves are usually sold across multiple European markets where Nutri-Score is voluntary or recommended (France, Belgium, Germany, Spain, Netherlands, Switzerland, Luxembourg).
rows.page_title: Front-of-Pack Systems, Decoded — Traffic Lights, RI, Nutri-Score · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Per 100g column
rows.kip: Mandatory under FIR for solids. Lets you compare any two products like-for-like.
rows.page_title: Front-of-Pack Systems, Decoded — Traffic Lights, RI, Nutri-Score · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: \"Gluten-free\"
rows.what: Less than 20 ppm (mg/kg) of gluten in the finished product
rows.kip: The Codex Alimentarius / EU 828/2014 threshold. Verified by competitive ELISA testing (R5 antibody method is the standard). Safe for the great majority of people with coeliac disease.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Wheat
rows.what: All wheat varieties — common, durum, spelt, kamut, einkorn, emmer, triticale (wheat × rye)
rows.kip: Direct gluten source. All varieties contain gluten.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Barley
rows.what: Cereal grain — in beer, malt, soups, savoury bases
rows.kip: Contains gluten (technically hordein). Direct source.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Rye
rows.what: Cereal grain — rye bread, crispbreads, pumpernickel
rows.kip: Contains gluten (technically secalin). Direct source.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Triticale
rows.kip: Contains gluten from both parents.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Rice (all varieties)
rows.what: White, brown, basmati, jasmine, wild rice
rows.kip: Naturally gluten-free. Cross-contamination risk when processed alongside gluten-containing grains; certified gluten-free rice is available.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Maize / corn
rows.kip: Naturally gluten-free. Watch for processed corn products combined with wheat (e.g., some breakfast cereals).
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Buckwheat
rows.what: Despite the name, not wheat — a pseudo-cereal
rows.kip: Naturally gluten-free. Watch for cross-contamination if processed in a facility that handles wheat.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Quinoa, amaranth, millet, sorghum, teff
rows.kip: All naturally gluten-free. Common in gluten-free baking and meal alternatives.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Potato, tapioca, cassava, arrowroot starch
rows.kip: Naturally gluten-free. Used as wheat-flour alternatives in baking.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Pulses and legume flours (chickpea, lentil, pea)
rows.what: Naturally gluten-free protein- and fibre-rich flours
rows.kip: Naturally gluten-free. Common in gluten-free baking, falafel, ready meals.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Oats (standard)
rows.kip: Standard oats and oat products are typically grown, harvested, milled, or processed alongside wheat, barley, or rye. Not safe for coeliac unless specifically labelled gluten-free.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Gluten-free oats (Coeliac UK accredited)
rows.what: Oats from a controlled gluten-free supply chain
rows.kip: Grown, harvested, milled, and processed in a gluten-free environment. Tested below 20 ppm. Safe for the great majority of people with coeliac disease.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Avenin sensitivity in oats
rows.what: A small subset of coeliacs react to avenin, the gluten-like protein in oats
rows.kip: About 1–5% of people with coeliac disease react to pure gluten-free oats. NICE recommends introducing oats cautiously and monitoring symptoms after diagnosis.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Malt vinegar
rows.kip: Coeliac UK considers malt vinegar safe for coeliac disease — the gluten level after fermentation is below 20 ppm. People with wheat allergy may still need to avoid it. (Distilled vinegar is always safe.)
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Tamari
rows.what: Japanese soy sauce traditionally made without wheat
rows.kip: Most tamari is wheat-free, but some commercial brands include wheat. Always check the label and look for a gluten-free claim.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Modified wheat starch
rows.kip: Generally contains gluten unless specifically labelled gluten-free wheat starch (a Codex-permitted gluten-free ingredient processed to remove gluten below 20 ppm).
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Hydrolysed wheat protein
rows.what: Wheat protein broken down into amino acids
rows.kip: Direct wheat/gluten source. Common in soups, sauces, gravies, ready meals.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Beer (standard)
rows.kip: Contains gluten. Gluten-free beers (made from sorghum, rice, millet, or specifically processed barley) are increasingly available.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Stock cubes / bouillon
rows.kip: Many commercial stock cubes contain wheat flour or barley malt extract. Check the bolded allergens. Gluten-free stock is widely available but not the default.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sausages and meat products
rows.what: Often contain rusk or breadcrumbs as a binder
rows.kip: Many UK sausages contain wheat-based rusk; meatballs and meatloaves often contain breadcrumbs. Always check — gluten-free sausages are available but not the default.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Communion wafers
rows.kip: Standard communion wafers contain gluten. Low-gluten and gluten-free communion options are available; check with the church or producer.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Some medicines and supplements
rows.what: Tablet binders sometimes contain wheat starch
rows.kip: Most tablet excipients are not wheat-derived but some products use wheat starch or modified wheat starch. Check with the pharmacist or producer if managing coeliac.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Pre-packed processed meats
rows.what: Glaze and coating may contain wheat
rows.kip: Some processed meats use wheat starch or wheat-based glazes. Check the bolded allergens.
rows.page_title: Gluten-Free, Decoded — Thresholds, Cross-Contamination, Hidden Sources · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: The 2% rule
rows.what: Ingredients below 2% of the finished product can appear in any order at the end of the list
rows.kip: FIR Article 18(2). Manufacturers can list small-quantity ingredients in any order after the main ingredients. Common for spices, vitamins, preservatives. Allergens still always declared.
rows.page_title: Ingredient List Rules, Decoded — How to Read Any Food Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Reconstituted water
rows.what: Water restored to a dehydrated ingredient is not counted as an ingredient
rows.kip: Water added during processing to rehydrate dried ingredients is not separately listed. This affects the descending-order calculation.
rows.page_title: Ingredient List Rules, Decoded — How to Read Any Food Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Added water above 5%
rows.what: Water added at more than 5% of the finished product must be declared
rows.kip: FIR Annex VII. Common in cured meats, where added water and brine are explicit ingredients. The water content can be substantial.
rows.page_title: Ingredient List Rules, Decoded — How to Read Any Food Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Bold, italic, or capital letters
rows.what: The 14 allergens must be visually distinguished
rows.kip: FIR Article 21. Allergens must stand out from surrounding text by typography (bold, italic, underlined, capitals) or by colour or background. Bold is the most common UK convention. Whatever the chosen method, it must be consistent.
rows.page_title: Ingredient List Rules, Decoded — How to Read Any Food Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Fish species naming
rows.what: Fish must be identified by species or commercial designation
rows.kip: Per Common Fisheries Policy retained law and FIR. The commercial designation, scientific name, production method (caught/farmed), and catch area must appear on most fish products.
rows.page_title: Ingredient List Rules, Decoded — How to Read Any Food Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Low energy / low calorie
rows.kip: Strict per-100g/ml threshold. Note the per-portion figure can be very different if the portion is small or large.
rows.page_title: Nutrition Claims, Decoded — What "Low Fat" Legally Means · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Energy free / calorie free
rows.kip: Reserved for drinks. Diet sodas typically meet this threshold. Solid foods cannot use this claim.
rows.page_title: Nutrition Claims, Decoded — What "Low Fat" Legally Means · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Low saturated fat / low saturates
rows.what: Threshold: ≤1.5g saturates/100g (solids); ≤0.75g/100ml (liquids); saturated + trans fat ≤10% energy
rows.kip: Two conditions must both be met: the absolute threshold AND the ≤10% energy from saturated + trans fat. Strict.
rows.page_title: Nutrition Claims, Decoded — What "Low Fat" Legally Means · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Saturated fat free / saturates free
rows.kip: Includes both saturates and trans fats in the limit.
rows.page_title: Nutrition Claims, Decoded — What "Low Fat" Legally Means · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Low sugar / low sugars
rows.kip: Includes all mono- and disaccharide sugars (sucrose, glucose, fructose, lactose), whether added or naturally present.
rows.page_title: Nutrition Claims, Decoded — What "Low Fat" Legally Means · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium-free / salt-free
rows.kip: Effectively zero. Strict threshold.
rows.page_title: Nutrition Claims, Decoded — What "Low Fat" Legally Means · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Source of fibre / contains fibre
rows.kip: Either condition can be met. Wholewheat bread typically meets this; many breakfast cereals meet it without being a meaningfully high-fibre source per portion.
rows.page_title: Nutrition Claims, Decoded — What "Low Fat" Legally Means · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: High in fibre / high fibre
rows.kip: Twice the source-of-fibre threshold. Wholegrain cereals, legumes, and high-fibre breads meet this.
rows.page_title: Nutrition Claims, Decoded — What "Low Fat" Legally Means · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Increased [nutrient]
rows.what: Threshold: ≥30% more than a comparable product (vitamins/minerals: ≥15% NRV)
rows.kip: Common on protein-fortified, fibre-fortified, or vitamin-fortified products. Comparator must be specified.
rows.page_title: Nutrition Claims, Decoded — What "Low Fat" Legally Means · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Natural
rows.what: For foods, defined under retained EU rules and enforcement guidance
rows.kip: Permitted for foods produced from natural ingredients with minimal processing — no artificial additives, no significant chemical modification. ASA and Trading Standards rule on misuse case-by-case.
rows.page_title: Nutrition Claims, Decoded — What "Low Fat" Legally Means · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium chloride
rows.kip: The baseline. All other sodium sources add to this total.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sea salt
rows.what: Evaporated seawater — same sodium as table salt
rows.kip: Same sodium content as table salt. Mineral traces negligible at food quantities.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Rock salt
rows.what: Mined salt — same sodium as table salt
rows.kip: Same as table salt. Still salt.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Celery salt
rows.kip: Primarily sodium chloride. Still contributes to daily sodium total.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Garlic salt
rows.kip: Primarily sodium chloride. Still contributes to daily sodium total.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Onion salt
rows.kip: Primarily sodium chloride. Still salt.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium bicarbonate (E500)
rows.kip: Contributes sodium. Common in bread and baked goods. Still adds to daily total.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium carbonate (E500ii)
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Less common than bicarbonate but still counts.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium hydroxide (E524)
rows.what: Lye — acidity regulator in pretzels and olives
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Trace amounts after processing.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium sulphate (E514)
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Uncommon in food.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium acetate (E262)
rows.what: Acidity regulator — salt and vinegar flavour
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Common in crisps and snacks.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium lactate (E325)
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Common in processed meat.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Disodium guanylate (E627)
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Used in combination with MSG. Purine concern for gout sufferers.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Disodium inosinate (E631)
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Purine compound. Often animal-derived.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Yeast extract
rows.what: Concentrated yeast — high in natural glutamates and sodium
rows.kip: Very high sodium content. Used as a flavour base in ready meals, soups, and snacks. Often appears lower on ingredients list than its sodium contribution suggests.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Hydrolysed vegetable protein (HVP)
rows.what: Protein broken down into amino acids — umami and sodium
rows.kip: High sodium. Used as a cheap flavour base. Sometimes contains MSG formed during hydrolysis.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Hydrolysed soy protein
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Soy allergen. Common in Asian-style sauces and ready meals.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Autolysed yeast extract
rows.kip: High sodium. Similar to yeast extract. Common in soups and snack seasonings.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium caseinate
rows.what: Milk protein used as emulsifier and flavour carrier
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Dairy-derived. Hidden in non-dairy creamers and processed foods.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium benzoate (E211)
rows.what: Preservative in soft drinks and sauces
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Also forms benzene with ascorbic acid in acidic drinks. Double reason to note this one.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium nitrite (E250)
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Also linked to nitrosamine formation. Worth flagging on both counts.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium nitrate (E251)
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Nitrosamine pathway. Worth flagging.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium metabisulphite (E223)
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Sulphite allergen — must be declared if >10ppm. Asthma trigger.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium sulphite (E221)
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Sulphite sensitivity concern.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium hydrogen sulphite (E222)
rows.what: Preservative in wine and dried fruit
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Sulphite allergen.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium propionate (E281)
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Low sodium content per serving but adds to total.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium sorbate
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Less common than potassium sorbate.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium erythorbate (E316)
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Often used alongside sodium nitrite in processed meat.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Brine
rows.what: Salt water — used in canning and pickling
rows.kip: Very high sodium. Canned vegetables, olives, and fish in brine can be significantly saltier than their fresh equivalents. Drain and rinse.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Soy sauce
rows.kip: One tablespoon contains around 1g of salt. Easy to exceed daily limits with soy-based sauces and marinades.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Fish sauce
rows.kip: One tablespoon can contain 1.5–2g of salt. Extremely high sodium density.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Worcestershire sauce
rows.what: Fermented condiment — moderate to high sodium
rows.kip: Moderate sodium per serving but used liberally. Check label.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Stock / bouillon
rows.what: Concentrated flavour base — very high sodium
rows.kip: Stock cubes and bouillon are very high in sodium. One cube can contain 1–2g of salt.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Miso
rows.kip: High sodium but fermented — some evidence of probiotic benefit. Moderation advised.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Smoked foods
rows.what: Salt used in smoking process — contributes sodium
rows.kip: Smoking often involves salting. Smoked fish, meat, and cheese can be high in sodium.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Cheese
rows.kip: Hard cheeses and processed cheese slices are high in sodium. Cottage cheese and ricotta are lower.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Monosodium phosphate
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Standard in processed cheese slices and cheese spreads.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Disodium phosphate (E339)
rows.what: Emulsifier in processed cheese and ready meals
rows.kip: Sodium and phosphate contributing. Common in instant noodles and processed cheese.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Trisodium phosphate
rows.what: Acidity regulator — also an industrial cleaning agent
rows.kip: Sodium and phosphate contributing. Found in some breakfast cereals.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium citrate (E331)
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Common in fizzy drinks and processed cheese.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium alginate (E401)
rows.what: Thickener in plant milks and sauces
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Derived from seaweed. Generally low sodium contribution.
rows.page_title: Hidden Names for Salt, Decoded — What Salt Really Looks Like on a Label · SCANSMART Knowledge Library

rows.name: Sodium stearoyl lactylate (E481)
rows.kip: Sodium-contributing. Common in industrial bread. Low sodium content per serving.
